chapters.title: Living side by side in different worlds
chapters.text:
Each person carries their own mix of values, priorities, thoughts, and growth. Together they shape how we act, lead, and talk. That mix is called a paradigm of thinking. As people and societies change, paradigms change too. This idea lives inside a model called integral dynamics, which is the base for the ideas in this summary.

Integral dynamics says that our thinking grows in stages, and each stage shifts what we care about. No single paradigm is better than another. Each one shines in its own setting.

A paradigm is the set of core values and habits that drive how we think. It shapes our actions.

Paradigms live in people, firms, and even whole countries. They mirror the dominant view of the group. That is why different workplaces and leadership styles fit different paradigms. The next sections look at well known paradigms and how they shape work.

So why does any of this matter? The short answer is to build understanding. Crossing the gap between people with different paradigms can be hard. We share space but live in different worlds, because paradigms act as quiet filters that shape what we see.

Each paradigm gives a full and steady view of the world, a complete take on what exists and what happens. Until a key moment, no one spots the flaws in their own view when talking to someone from another view, as author Valerii Pekar notes.

From birth, we move through these stages. Most people pass through some, but not all. The path depends on life events and personal growth. Philosopher Ken Wilber paints a picture of a wide river. We start at the same bank, but we end in different places. Our tools and skills shape where we land. Some swim, some paddle, and some fear the water.

chapters.title: The earliest stages of human thought
chapters.text:
For the sake of memory, this summary uses colors to mark each paradigm. There are six clear paradigms so far, a seventh on the horizon, and an eighth that is still a theory.

Humanity keeps evolving in how it thinks, the same way Charles Darwin described for the body. That evolution brings new paradigms.

The earliest stage is the beige world, which runs on pure instinct. Survival is the only goal in a harsh setting where basic needs are tough to meet. People in deep crisis, like homelessness, addiction, or severe brain injury, often live in this paradigm. About 0.1 percent of adults sit here. War, disaster, and bad leaders can push anyone into it. Infants touch this stage briefly before moving on to purple.

The purple world feels safer and runs on shared rules that meet the group needs. People in this stage often believe in magic and ritual. They follow many rules and have a weak sense of self. The group matters more than the person, and loyalty rules. About 10 percent of people live here. The pull of a calm childhood is strong, but staying too long holds you back.

The next stage is the red world. Red turns the focus back to the self and the ego. People here chase fast pleasure and prize strength, cunning, and power. This stage grew up with farming, when food and goods gave the ability to sway others. About 20 percent of the global population sits in red. Teens, tyrants, criminals, corrupt cops, and shady politicians all show up here. Every child passes through a red phase when they test the limits of self. Red may look harsh, but it is a normal part of growth and not a flaw.

chapters.title: When new problems bring new views
chapters.text:
A big shift in thinking comes with the blue world. Here people look for peace and a higher purpose, guided by firm rules and a system of reward and punishment. Blue prizes morality, law, and order. Deep faith or strong ideology drive it, and the focus is on how one believes more than what one believes. Blue covers about 40 percent of people, which makes it the most common paradigm.

When blue order blocks progress, the orange paradigm steps in. Orange acts like a machine. It is fully rational and offers plenty of chances to grow. Orange has no single truth, but it always offers a choice. Success rules the day, and material signs of status matter most. Founders, scientists, and artists often live here. Rooted in the Enlightenment and fed by the Industrial Revolution, orange covers about 30 percent of people today.

There are more blues in the world than oranges, yet almost all wealth and power sit with the orange crowd. The green world is newer and stands for harmony, humanism, spirit, and care for others. Green puts healthy relationships ahead of personal success. Its people welcome differences and push for equal worth. Movements like environmentalism, social justice, and equal rights all carry a green tone. Less than 10 percent of people live here.

Green is great in many ways, but it moves slowly. That pace pushes restless doers into the yellow world. Yellow is a complex second order realm that blends the strengths of all the earlier stages. Yellow people adapt fast, shift roles, and switch views with ease. Independence, dignity, and freedom are the core values. Yellows aim to grow themselves and help others. They solve problems fast and think big. Only 1 percent of people reach yellow, and they stand out for their mix of charm and skill.

chapters.title: Why growth and movement never stop
chapters.text:
Now that the paradigms are clearer, the next question is how people, firms, and even whole nations move along this rainbow. The shift from one world to the next is slow, not a quick jump. This section looks at the path between worlds and the general pattern of change.

One key pattern stands out. Paradigms move in a zigzag, swinging back and forth between the group and the self, and between the material and the ideal. Beige focuses on self survival, purple on the group. Red swings back to the self, blue to the group. The swing keeps going, but each loop reaches a higher, more complex level.

More complex life often comes with growth. The color worlds rarely show up in pure form. They mix and overlap. Moving from one to the next is hard, which is why only about a third of people reach orange. Some do not feel the push to grow, while others lack the chance.

Still, the march forward is vital. Each new stage gives tools to handle old problems and face new ones.

You cannot solve a problem on the same level that it was created. You have to rise above it to the next level, as Albert Einstein put it. No matter where you stand on the path, you do not have to tick off each world like a list. Older paradigms stay useful, like rings in a tree trunk. They live in the same mind and support each other. It is wise to learn how to call on each one when it fits. You can draw on red when facing a robber and blue when you need to set order.

The trick is to know each paradigm and learn to switch them on when needed. By the way, the color names follow a pattern. Warm shades mark individualistic worlds, while cool shades mark those that lean toward the group.

chapters.title: Matching people to the right work
chapters.text:
The world is full of colors, and each paradigm asks for its own approach. Adapting to the colors around you can be tough, especially inside teams and firms. Each paradigm needs a different style of teaching, goal setting, and motivation. A good leader reads those needs and sets people up to do their best. That is how a company hits peak output.

Rule one is simple. Not everyone is built for the spotlight. Each person has their own mix of skills, knowledge, and traits, and the paradigm shapes it most. The split is rough, but it helps us sort the colors.

Everyone should do the work that matches their nature, and employees in the right role enjoy their job, as Valerii Pekar writes. Different people need different handling. Red workers do best with clear simple tasks and do not bring much personal drive. They respond to firm correction. Blue workers follow detailed tasks without much oversight. They need only a mentor, and they thrive on praise. Orange workers can handle complex projects on their own. Give them a goal, the right tools, and room to find their way. They like to innovate and value feedback. Green workers excel at long term goals and big team projects. They are rare, so hiring them is hard. Yellow workers pick their own goals and direction, which makes them tough to manage. Their output depends on the fit with the work. Purple workers tend to react to the task in front of them. They are rare and form the core of farming, but they can be tough to lead.

When a manager knows what each color can do, they can place people in the right seat. The right fit lets any worker show their strengths and add real value to the team. There is no single best paradigm. Every firm needs a mix of types across its teams and stages.

chapters.title: The right culture for the right team
chapters.text:
Just as people carry their own paradigm, so do firms and groups. This section looks at the culture of a company, the paradigm inside its walls.

The culture of a firm is the set of principles, norms, and rules its people follow. When a firm culture clashes with your paradigm, even a great job can feel wrong. Comfort and fit matter as much as pay and status. The next paragraphs cover the main types of management culture, so you can spot the one that fits you or your team.

Purple organizations are rare, much like purple workers. They build a strong sense of belonging, almost like a family, with a clear leader and tight informal bonds. This style shows up in small family firms and new start ups, where trust and devotion drive the work.

Red organizations run on raw power. One strong leader makes the calls and rules the structure. There is no real democracy, and the whole firm depends on that one person. Red works in a crisis, but it blocks growth and learning over time.

Blue organizations rely on clear chains of command and firm rules. These bureaucratic groups set strict steps for every process. Blue fits large institutions like churches, government agencies, and firms in stable fields. The setting is safe and the path up the ladder is clear.

Orange organizations chase success and prize results over hierarchy. These modern firms value the employee experience and feed off a shared drive to win. Every voice counts, and everyone shapes the call. Orange teams are flexible, fast, and ready to adapt.

Green organizations live in the non profit world. They have no formal hierarchy, and each voice carries equal weight. A unique feature is shared leadership, where team members take turns at the top. The base of these teams is harmony, deep trust, and the joy of working well together.

chapters.title: A world made richer by variety
chapters.text:
Where can we find the perfect leader? Integral dynamics says there is none. Each style has its own fit, and it lines up with certain situations, firms, types of workers, and stages of growth.

The world is richer because it is varied, and the colorful paradigms are a clear sign of that mix. To live a vivid, full life, we have to welcome variety, both inside and out.

Integral dynamics shows up in work and in personal growth, but the reach is much wider. Politics, the economy, the military, faith, and identity all feel the pull of paradigms. Human thought keeps moving, and that motion drives real change in every corner of life. Watching the shift is useful, because it makes sense of what is happening around us and points to the next step.

We sit high on the ladder of evolution, but the climb is not over. Human awareness keeps growing. Each push for self growth adds a small piece to the larger rise of the universe. That is reason enough to keep aiming high.

A useful first step is to think about the paradigms that shape your own life and actions. Rate the impact of each color on you and your surroundings on a scale of one to ten. The biggest gaps between you and your world are often the source of discomfort.
